title: AI Crawler Observatory · Italy
text:
Block
Sites keep AI crawlers out through robots.txt — a plain-text file that can name a crawler and disallow paths. Where that happens is uneven: below, the share of each sector’s sites that block GPTBot by name.

Crawler by crawler, the picture is the same one seen from the other side. Each bar is one crawler and splits into how the 543 sites treat it: blocked at the root, partial (some paths off limits), or allowed. Every bar is a whole, so the figures inside it are shares of sites, not counts — the counts are in the tooltip and the table. Use the menus to slice by sector, or by whether a rule names the crawler or comes from a catch-all *.

title: AI Crawler Observatory · Italy
text:
Offer
The opposite move. llms.txt (a 2024 convention) is a Markdown file that offers a curated view of a site to language models — “here is what to read about us.” Almost nobody uses it: 46 of 543 sites, 8.5%. And the lead is an unexpected sector — banking, where more than 1 in 4 sites publish one, and every one is hand-written, not shipped by a plugin.
The chart below counts only those 46 publishers — not the whole sample — and splits each sector’s bar by where the file came from: ink for a hand-written file, gray for a plugin default. That split is the question this section asks: how much of the adoption was a decision rather than a side effect. Sector sample sizes are in the table.

title: AI Crawler Observatory · Italy
text:
Reserve
The legal one. Under EU copyright law (Directive 2019/790, Article 4), AI companies may text-and-data-mine public content for training unless the owner reserves the right in a machine-readable form. TDMRep is that reservation — the only one of the three signals with legal weight in the EU. Almost nobody uses it, and those who do live on copyright.


8of 543 sitesPublishers & universities 6 · News 2 · every other sector 0

title: AI Crawler Observatory · Italy
text:
Method
The sample. 543 Italian websites, stratified across 10 sectors, drawn from hand-curated sector lists cross-checked against the Tranco top-1M list filtered to .it. Latest snapshot: 2026-07-14. The observatory is built to re-run: each crawl is one snapshot, so the same measures can be tracked over time and, later, across countries.
The three signals. For every site we fetch and validate three files:

robots.txt, parsed under RFC 9309 (longest-match, Allow wins ties). A crawler’s verdict is blocked when the root path is denied, partial when only some paths are, allowed otherwise. A rule is specific when the site names the crawler, wildcard when it comes from a catch-all User-agent: *.
llms.txt, a Markdown file offering content to models. Hand-written vs plugin default is inferred from generator signatures and near-duplicate detection across sites.
tdmrep.json / TDMRep, the EU legal reservation, read through all three spec channels (a well-known file, HTTP headers, and HTML meta tags).

Presence is validated, never assumed. A 200 OK proves nothing — many servers answer 200 with an HTML page on any path. A file counts only if it is structurally valid; soft-404s are excluded.
Limits. robots.txt measures declared intent, not crawler behaviour. The CMS/plugin fingerprint is heuristic. “Not mentioned” is a default, not a decision, and is kept separate from explicit permission throughout.
Data & code. 20 AI crawlers are tracked. The full pipeline and the aggregated snapshot data are open: github.com/mxdangelo/aicrawl-census.
